heading: GLI INTERVALLI DEI GENERI PRIMARI
card_summary: This long chapter from the fifth "Tono" of the first "Cantico" expounds the Pythagorean-Platonic musical cosmology of the Timaeus, tracing how the soul and world are structured through the lambda-series (1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 9, 27) and explaining why arithmetic, geometric, and harmonic means must fill the intervals between those limits. Zorzi draws heavily on Proclus's commentary on the Timaeus, as well as on Calcidius, Porphyry, Adrastus, Severus, Ptolemy, Aristoxenus, Timaeus of Locri, Boethius, Nicomachus, and Orphic theology. The chapter pivots from mathematical music theory to Kabbalistic gematria when Zorzi shows that Metatron (sar ha-panim, "prince of the face"), the first and most perfect of all creatures, encodes the number 999 — three nines — in its Hebrew letters, a number corresponding to the cube of 9 (27) and to the divine name Adonai Tzevaot; he also equates Metatron numerically with the Virgin Mary. The macrocosmic number-scheme is then mirrored in the human microcosm, where three novenaries correspond to body, soul, and intellect respectively, each terminated in a tenth that points toward the divine unity.
essay:
# GLI INTERVALLI DEI GENERI PRIMARI — Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi*, pp. 677–728

*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of the chapter (approximately the first 6,000 words / roughly chapters I–X of the section). The full section extends to p. 728 and likely continues the macrocosm-microcosm argument and further Kabbalistic applications; those later passages are not reflected here.*

---

## I. The Platonic Lambda and the Musical Structure of Soul and World (Cap. I)

Zorzi opens by affirming what "all Pythagoreans and Academics" know: that both the world and the soul were described first by **Timaeus of Locri** and then by **Plato** (in the *Timaeus*) according to musical proportions, using a series beginning with unity, doubling to the cube of 2 (1, 2, 4, 8) and tripling to the cube of 3 (1, 3, 9, 27). These are the seven intervals of an eight-stringed instrument. The even numbers express the "feminine" principle, the odd numbers the "masculine," and from their combination all things are generated. The upper limit of the entire structure is the cube of each series, because spatial reality cannot go beyond three dimensions (length, breadth, depth).

Zorzi surveys the graphical representations of this series: **Adrastus** and **Calcidius** arrange the numbers in a triangle with unity at the apex; **Porphyry**, **Severus**, and **Proclus** prefer three columns; Proclus, following Plato most closely, collapses all seven terms onto a single line: 1. 2. 3. 4. 8. 9. 27.

---

## II. Which Intervals Can and Cannot Be Filled (Cap. II)

Not all the intervals between the lambda-series terms can be harmonically filled. The interval between 1 and 2 has no mean unless unity is split — a procedure Proclus mentions but rejects. Between 2 and 4, the only middle term is 3, which gives a *sesquialtera* ratio (3:2) to the lower and a *subsesquitertia* (4:3) to the upper — insufficient for harmonic completion. Similarly, between 4 and 8 sits 6, with equivalent shortcomings. On the odd side (the triple series), between 1 and 3 sits 2 (double to 1, sesquialtera to 3); between 3 and 9 sits 6 (double to 3, sesquialtera to 9); between 9 and 27 sits 18 with the same proportions.

> *Inter duo autem, et quatuor intervenit solus ternarius cum proportione sesqualtera ad minorem numerum, et subsesquitertia ad maiorem, unde neque hic potest harmonice compleri intervallum.*

<cite>p. 510 (Latin text, Cap. II)</cite>

---

## III. Extension to Larger Numbers: The Series Rooted in 6 (Cap. III)

Since the 27-series cannot accommodate all primary genera, Zorzi (following Proclus) proposes a second figure using the same proportions but a wider field: take **6** as the new unity, double to 12, 24, 48, and triple to 18, 54, 162. In this expanded scheme all intervals can be properly filled. Between 6 and 12, the arithmetic mean is 9 and the harmonic mean is 8; between 12 and 24, the means are 18 (harmonic) and 16 (arithmetic). The interval ratios produce the complete system of consonances: the octave (*diapason*) from the double ratio, the fifth (*diapente*) from the sesquialtera (3:2), the fourth (*diatessaron*) from the sesquitertia (4:3), and the tone (*tonus*) from the sesquioctava (9:8).

Zorzi gives detailed calculations of arithmetic, geometric, and harmonic means following **Proclus**'s derivation. For the harmonic mean: take the difference of the extremes (6), multiply by the lesser extreme (6), divide by their sum (18), obtaining 2; add to the lesser extreme to get 8.

> *Medietas quippe arithmetica est proportio excessuum, geometrica vero est proportio proportionum, ex quibus ambabus resultat harmonica.*

<cite>p. 512 (Latin text, Cap. III)</cite>

---

## IV. Further Extension to the Maximum Numbers: 384 to 10368 (Cap. IV)

Still following **Proclus**, Zorzi explains why the system must be enlarged again: to divide the sesquitertia intervals into sesquioctave ratios and *lemmata* (minor semitones), the numbers must be scaled up. Taking 64 (the third power of 2 from unity, i.e. 8 × 8 = 64 under octuple proportion), tripled to 192, the analysis proceeds through 192, 216, 243, 256, 288, 384. Because 288 divided by 8 yields 40.5 (a fraction), all numbers must be doubled, giving the canonical series: **384, 432, 486, 512, 576, 648, 729, 768**. The full seven-term series then extends from 384 to 10368.

The harmonic and arithmetic means are computed for each double and triple interval: 384–768 (means: 512 harmonic, 576 arithmetic); 384–1152 (576 harmonic, 768 arithmetic); up to 3456–10368 (5184 harmonic, 6912 arithmetic). The entire sequence yields 34 terms, whose total consonance amounts to four octaves plus a fifth plus a tone (*quater diapason cum diapente et tono*), confirming that the series ends on a tone rather than on the final lemma (contrary to **Severus**'s interpretation).

> *In qua serie trigintaquatuor termini habentur, quorum consonantia est quater diapason, cum diapente, et tono, quamvis Severus in lemma ipsam figuram terminet, sed clarius, et lucidius colligitur terminari in tonum.*

<cite>p. 520 (Latin text, between Cap. IV and Cap. V)</cite>

---

## V. Why Plato Used the Diatonic Genus (Cap. V)

The three ancient genera of harmony are: (1) **diatonic** (semitone + tone + tone), (2) **enharmonic** (*diesis* + *diesis* + *ditonus*), (3) **chromatic** (semitone + semitone + *trisemitonium*). Zorzi notes that the Pythagoreans rejected the semitone as incapable of equal division, though later theorists — especially **Ptolemy** — divided the tone into unequal semitones (*lemma* and *apotome*).

Plato chose the diatonic genus because it governs rational life; the enharmonic belongs to the life shared with all bodies, and the chromatic to corporeal existence as such. **Aristoxenus**'s claim that the ancients did not know the diatonic is dismissed as the polemical showmanship noted by **Adrastus**.

---

## VI. Metatron, Gematria, and the Three Nines (Cap. VI)

The chapter pivots dramatically from Platonic music theory into **Kabbalistic gematria**. Zorzi introduces the doctrine — among "the most recondite arcana of the Hebrews" — that there is one first of all creatures, containing the perfection of all others: **Metatron** (*sar ha-panim*, "prince of the face"), who stands always before the Emperor (God) and introduces the worthy to His presence. According to Hebrew tradition, it was Metatron who spoke with Moses and elevated him to converse with the Creator face to face.

The numerical value of the letters of Metatron's name (in Hebrew: מיטטרון) equals **999**: nine hundreds, nine tens, nine units — three nines. Zorzi reads this as the containment of all creaturely virtue: simpler entities expressed in units, intermediate ones in tens, composite ones in hundreds. All terminate in the authentic tenth, which is **aleph** (the letter also meaning 1,000), the true unity, first, and last. He cites the end of the Song of Songs — *"aleph tibi Salomone, tibi qui es pax"* — to show that Metatron, as first creature, is not its own end but points toward the Thousand/Aleph, which is God, who says in Isaiah: *"Ego sum primus et novissimus."*

> *Inter reconditissima arcana Hebraeorum hoc celeberrimum habetur, quod una est prima omnium creaturarum, ipsarum omnium perfectionem continens, quam vocant… mitatron principem facierum, quia semper stat ante faciem Imperatoris.*

<cite>p. 524 (Latin text, Cap. VI)</cite>

Crucially, Zorzi argues that Metatron is a name of *function*, not of person, and therefore **can apply to the Virgin Mary**: computing the final letters of the three words *Metatron sar ha-panim* yields 290 (nun = 50, mem = 40, resh = 200), and the Hebrew letters of Mary's name (מרים: mem = 40+40, resh = 200, yod = 10) also sum to 290. He is careful to say this does not mean Mary spoke to Moses; rather, whoever fills the Metatron role at any given moment does so by divine mandate, as Paul states in Galatians: *"Lex ordinata est per angelos in manu mediatoris."*

---

## VII. The Same Number 999 in the Divine Name and the Elements (Cap. VII)

Zorzi extends the gematria: after describing the creation of heaven and earth, Moses calls God *Adonai Tzevaot* ("Lord of Hosts," Hebrew: יהוה צבאות). The letter-values of this title sum to 999. Furthermore, the fully spelled-out letters of the divine Tetragrammaton combined with the letters for the four elements (fire, water, air/spirit, dust) also yield exactly 999. Zorzi names this technique **gematria** (גמטריה), calling it a branch of "secretior theologia," and credits **Pythagoras** with imitating this Hebrew method — while warning that those who pursue pure numbers without knowledge of the underlying mysteries waste their efforts.

---

## VIII. Unity as the Source of All Consonance (Cap. VIII)

Zorzi argues philosophically that all created things, scattered into many portions, would remain discordant unless reduced to unity through proper harmony. Drawing on **Boethius** and **Nicomachus**'s definition of consonance as "the concord of equal and unequal voices reduced to one," and on **Plato**'s *Parmenides*, he asserts that all things are "one" by participation in the supreme Unity. **Aristotle**'s objection to this thesis is, as **Simplicius** notes, a quarrel about words rather than meaning. **Orpheus** taught that God is one by identity, while all other things are said to be one by derivation, participation, and connection with the true One.

> *Consonantia enim (ut Boetius, et Nicomachus diffiniunt) est parium, dispariumque vocum in unum redacta concordia.*

<cite>p. 528 (Italian translation, Cap. VIII)</cite>

Proclus's *Theological Propositions* support this: all things, as they proceeded from the One, hasten back to it with continuous motion. Dionysius the Areopagite adds that the Creator, as cause of all, draws all things back to himself lest they fail or be disturbed when left to themselves (*Ps.* 104:29 is cited).

---

## IX. The Heptachord and the Human Soul (Cap. IX)

Zorzi turns to the application of the Platonic heptachord to the human soul specifically. The soul is not a mathematical number abstracted from corporeal law; it is a *substantial*, *uniform*, *self-reflexive*, and *rational* number, immeasurably superior to matter. Quoting Proclus again, he argues the soul's division derives not from matter but from the efficient cause, on the model of intelligible exemplars. The soul is both divisible and indivisible (Plato's *Timaeus*): Aristotle correctly notes that even in divisible things there is something indivisible that holds them together, and this applies much more to the soul. The Creator made the soul as a whole before its parts; the whole precedes the parts essentially (not integrally).

Zorzi then introduces the Orphic-Neoplatonic mythology of **Dionysus** and **Apollo** as the nocturnal-dissociating and diurnal-unifying aspects of the divine solar principle. Dionysus, cut into seven parts at night, represents the soul's descent and fragmentation; Apollo (whose name is read as *a-pellon*, "he who drives away evils," or Athenian *Alexikakos*, or Homeric *Ulion* "giver of health") represents reintegration. The number seven (= 3 + 4 = first odd + first even) is the "leader of the heptad," producing the *bis-diapason* from 1 + 2 + 4 — the "most perfect harmony," which is why Apollo carries the lyre.

---

## X. The Three Nines in the Human Microcosm (Cap. X)

The chapter culminates in the microcosmic application: man, "the most perfect image of the world," contains the same three nines in scaled proportions.

1. **First ninety** (novenarius of hundreds): the body, which is dual (corruptible + incorruptible after resurrection). Each half is composed of four elements (Plato's *Timaeus*): 4 + 4 = 8, plus the single animating life (*nephesh*, Hebrew נפש) = 9, completed in the soul as tenth.
2. **Second ninety** (novenarius of tens): in the soul, formed by the five external senses + four internal faculties (imaginative, cogitative, phantasia, sensus communis) = 9, completed in the intellect as tenth.
3. **Third ninety** (novenarius of units): in the intellect itself, corresponding to the nine orders of angels and the higher divine enumerations, completed in the supreme Fountain of all — addressed in prayer as "Our Father," per Christ's command.

---

## Sources and Technical Vocabulary

**Ancient**: Timaeus of Locri, Plato (*Timaeus*, *Parmenides*), Aristotle, Aristoxenus, Adrastus, Porphyry, Severus, Proclus (*In Timaeum*, *Theologica*, *Institutio Theologica*), Ptolemy, Calcidius, Nicomachus, Boethius, Orpheus (via Proclus), Hesiod, Euripides, Aeschylus, Homer, Simplicius, Pseudo-Dionysius.

**Scriptural**: Song of Songs 8:12, Isaiah (multiple), Genesis 2:1, Psalm 104:29–31, Galatians 3:19.

**Kabbalistic**: Metatron (*sar ha-panim*), *gematria* (גמטריה), *nephesh* (נפש), the Tetragrammaton fully spelled (*yod-he-vav-he*), *Adonai Tzevaot*, *aleph* as 1000.

**Technical musical terms**: *diapason* (octave), *diapente* (fifth), *diatessaron* (fourth), *tonus*, *lemma* (minor semitone), *apotome* (major semitone), *diesis*, *sesquialtera* (3:2), *sesquitertia* (4:3), *sesquioctava* (9:8), *diatonic/chromatic/enharmonic genera*, arithmetic / geometric / harmonic means.

---

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

This section is central to the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s synthesis: Zorzi weaves together the Platonic musical cosmos (the lambda series and its proportional means), Neoplatonic henology (everything proceeding from and returning to the One), and Christian Kabbalah (Metatron = 999 = three nines = cosmic cube = 27). The pivot from mathematics to gematria is characteristic of Zorzi's method throughout the work: numerical structures are simultaneously musical, cosmological, and scriptural. The identification of Metatron with the Virgin Mary (both yielding 290) is a signature move integrating Hebrew esotericism with Marian theology. The three-nine structure of the human microcosm (body/soul/intellect) parallels the three Sephirotic registers and anticipates the later discussion of angelic hierarchies.
